source.definition: To open the mouth wide, usually as a sign of tiredness or boredom.

explanation.sourceText: The English verb “yawn” originates from an imitative sound representing the act of opening the mouth wide, reflecting the natural human expression of tiredness or boredom.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „yawn“ provine dintr-o imitare a sunetului care reprezintă deschiderea largă a gurii, reflectând expresia naturală umană de oboseală sau plictiseală.

target.definition: A deschide gura larg, adesea ca semn de oboseală sau plictiseală.

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb “a căsca” is an onomatopoeic formation that imitates the sound and movement of opening the mouth.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a căsca“ este o formă onomatopeică care imită sunetul și mișcarea deschiderii gurii.

examples.sourceText: I yawn because I am tired.

examples.targetText: El căsca des în timpul orei.

examples.sourceText: Please don’t yawn while you are speaking!
examples.targetText: Te rog, nu căsca când vorbești!

examples.sourceText: The baby yawns and falls asleep.

learningTips.text: Remember that “a căsca” sounds like the noise you make when you yawn—an easy way to recall the verb.
The verb ends with -a, typical for Romanian first conjugation verbs—practice its forms to get comfortable.
Think of “a căsca” as an onomatopoeic word, just like the English “yawn” mimics the sound of opening your mouth.
Imagine opening your mouth wide and saying “kaska”—this helps you remember the Romanian verb.
